html:
Edexcel International GCSE English Language Specification A, Paper 1 (4EA1/01) — Section B: Transactional Writing — 10 Most Likely Questions + Skeleton Plans (2026)

For: Pearson Edexcel International GCSE (9–1) English Language Specification A, Paper 1 (4EA1/01), Section B (Transactional Writing). This is the non-fiction paper: Section A pairs an anthology non-fiction extract with an unseen non-fiction text (reading); Section B then asks you to write one transactional piece. Section B offers a choice of two tasks — Question 6 OR Question 7 — and you answer ONLY ONE.

The marks — read this twice. Section B is worth 45 marks, split into two assessment objectives:

  • AO4 — Content and Organisation: 27 marks (60%). This is the quality of your writing: how well you communicate, how clearly you organise, how appropriate your register is for the form and audience, how persuasive and varied your language is.
  • AO5 — Technical Accuracy: 18 marks (40%). This is spelling, punctuation, grammar (SPaG), sentence-structure variety, and accurate vocabulary.

Do not confuse these labels with other boards. On the GCSE boards (AQA, Eduqas, etc.) the same two ideas are called AO5 (writing quality) and AO6 (technical accuracy). On Edexcel International GCSE Literature (4ET1), AO4 means Context — that does not apply here. For 4EA1/01 Language, AO4 = writing quality and AO5 = technical accuracy. Memorise it correctly: AO4 is the bigger one (27), and it is about the writing itself.

Word target: 700+ words. Spec A expects more on the page than the GCSE boards (which target around 650). The penalty for being under-length is severe — a thin answer cannot access the top AO4 bands because there is not enough writing to demonstrate sustained organisation. Aim for 720–750 words. The model answers below all land in that range.

Source. The themes below are Sophicly's verified 2026 forecast for 4EA1/01 Section B, distilled from the verified past-paper transactional-writing prompts (see The Complete Writer's Craft — Creative Non-Fiction, Verified Past-Paper Writing Prompts section, for the full table of statements, forms, and audiences). The forecast applies a recurrence-cycle analysis to Edexcel's own cluster pattern plus current-affairs salience for a 2026 candidate. Spec A is an international qualification, so examples are kept globally intelligible where possible; UK-anchored statistics are acceptable as illustrative evidence.

Statistics and sources. Every numeric claim and named source cited in the plans below has been verified against UK government, regulator, charity, or peer-reviewed sources (Ofcom, NHS, Sport England, WRAP, UNEP, UNWTO, Met Office, Mental Health Foundation, and similar). Examiners penalise invented statistics. Where a theme needs a figure that could not be verified, the claim is written in plain terms and marked [VERIFY-NEEDED] — never state an unverified number as fact.

Variety of openings — no two model answers start the same way. A top-band answer is not just a correctly structured IUMVCC; it opens in a way that is creative and fits the topic. There is no single "correct" opening. The model essays below deliberately use a different hook archetype each time, so you can see the range available to you — and so the document never teaches one formula. The Sophicly hooks are: a question, an "imagine" scenario, a metaphor, an anecdote, a shocking statistic, a bold statement, a contrast, an assumption-then-reversal, and repetition. Pick whichever genuinely suits the statement Edexcel prints — not whichever you rehearsed. Forcing a clever opening onto the wrong topic loses more than it gains; the opening must earn its place.

Opening-hook map — what each model answer demonstrates (so you see the full range):

Q Theme & form Hook archetype used What it demonstrates
Q1 Sport / competition (speech) Concrete contrast ("Picture two people…") Inequality made visible by placing two images side by side
Q2 Extreme sports / risk (article) Shocking statistic A single number that overturns the reader's assumption
Q3 Generational perception of teenagers (letter) Assumption set up, then reversed "You have been told… in fact the opposite is true"
Q4 Rules / values / behaviour (speech) Bold statement A claim with no qualifier, defended afterwards
Q5 Social media / online identity (article) Extended metaphor (the topic as a machine) A long, controlled sentence that builds one central image
Q6 School life / academic pressure (letter) Measured definition Calm, complete sentences that build a case brick by brick
Q7 Personal heroes / role models (speech) Personal anecdote A two-sentence memory that earns the argument
Q8 Travel / cultural experience (article) Extended metaphor (scale and appetite) A second metaphor opening — the technique flexes to a new topic
Q9 Environment / individual responsibility (speech) Repetition / anaphora "We choose. We refuse. We rebuild." — rhythm as persuasion
Q10 Appearance / image / judgement (letter) Historical contrast (then vs now) Two eras placed side by side to expose what has changed

These are illustrations, not prescriptions. The same topic could open with a rhetorical question, an imagine-scenario, or a one-line anecdote and score just as highly — provided the choice fits. Study the variety; copy the range, not any single example. Each essay still runs IUMVCC underneath. The architecture does not change; the opening does. That variety is what top-band AO4 (organisation that feels controlled and deliberate, not formulaic) rewards.

How to use this document. Each entry below gives:

  1. A predicted theme for 4EA1/01 Section B in 2026, with three plausible stem variants (different form / audience / statement combinations).
  2. A frame — the controlling argument that holds your whole answer together.
  3. Three hook options so you can pick the archetype that suits the stem Edexcel actually prints.
  4. A skeleton IUMVCC plan with sentence-level guidance for every beat.
  5. Three counter-argument variants so the strongest objection the surface prompt invites is the one you concede.
  6. A voice and banned-patterns check to run before you submit.

The plan structure stays identical across all ten questions. You rehearse the shell once; on the day, you swap in the surface prompt's audience, form, and statement of opinion. This is the same discipline that powers your Literature answers — memorise the load-bearing structure, recombine for the printed question.

Full IUMVCC walkthrough: Sophicly Persuasive Writing Guide.

Memorise this once — the IUMVCC plan shape

Sophicly's transactional persuasive structure has SIX beats. Memorise the beat name, the word target, and the dominant move for each one. (At Sophicly we use IUMVCC; the exam boards reward the outputs — clear organisation, sustained argument, variety — not the scaffold name.)

Beat Words Sentences Dominant move
I — Introduction 60–110 2–3 Hook + topic bridge. No facts list. Build an image.
U — Urgency 110–160 3–4 Metaphor-first. Why this matters NOW.
M — Methodology 280–380 9–12 (three sub-points) Each sub-point: point → metaphor → development → evidence. Organic transitions only.
V — Vision 110–160 3 Three escalating sentences painting the better future.
C — Counter-argument 80–110 2–3 Concede the strongest objection. Refute with one decisive pivot.
C — Conclusion 80–110 2–3 Circular echo of the Introduction image, or a climactic final picture.

Total on the page: ~720–760 words. This sits comfortably above the 700-word floor Spec A expects. AO4 reward = controlled, deliberate organisation that does not feel formulaic, plus persuasive language matched to form and audience. AO5 reward = controlled vocabulary, sentence-structure variety, accurate spelling and punctuation.

The nine Sophicly hooks (pick ONE per piece)

Edexcel reward variety; pick the archetype that suits the audience the stem specifies.

  1. Counterintuitive / shocking statistic — a number that overturns assumption.
  2. Personal anecdote — a two-sentence concrete memory.
  3. Sensory snapshot — a single image written like a paragraph from a novel.
  4. Imagine scenario — Picture this: + a future or hypothetical moment.
  5. Rhetorical question — a question only the reader can answer.
  6. Bold statement — a claim, no qualifier.
  7. Contrast / paradox — two clauses that crash against each other.
  8. Assumption-then-reversal — set up the expected view, then dismantle it.
  9. Extended metaphor / repetition — a single comparison the rest of the piece returns to, or an anaphora that builds pressure.

MADFATHER'S CROPS — the floor, not the ceiling

You need two or three devices working together at every persuasive moment. Single-device sentences score low on AO4. MADFATHER'S CROPS is an aide-memoire — a Sophicly scoring framework that lists the techniques you should never forget, not every technique you are allowed to use.

The MADFATHER'S CROPS aide-memoire:

  • M Metaphor / A Alliteration / D Direct address / F Facts + foreshadowing / A Assonance / T Triadic structure / H Hyperbole / E Emotive language / R Rhetorical questions / C Contrast / R Repetition / O Onomatopoeia / P Personification / S Simile + sibilance.

Beyond MADFATHER'S CROPS — the wider technique bank. Top-band AO4 answers reach for techniques the aide-memoire does not list. Learn the names and the shapes; use them where the topic invites them.

  • Anaphora — repeated word at the start of consecutive clauses ("We have the science. We have the funding. We have the time we are not using."). Repetition that builds pressure.
  • Epistrophe — repeated word at the END of consecutive clauses ("Reduce the waste. Repair the waste. Refuse the waste.").
  • Polysyndeton — repeated "and" (or another conjunction) connecting items ("The kit and the strip and the seat and the ticket and the broadcast."). Slows the reader down; emphasises accumulation.
  • Asyndeton — list with the conjunctions deliberately removed ("Reduce, repair, refuse."). Speeds the reader up; emphasises compression.
  • Polysyllabic listing — a list of multi-syllable words for cumulative weight ("relentless, industrialised, unregulated, profitable.").
  • Parallelism — same grammatical shape repeated ("Move the bell. Move the calendar. Move the system."). Anaphora plus parallelism is a top-band combination.
  • Antithesis — two ideas balanced against each other in matched grammar ("The market pays the wage; the wage pays the worker; the worker pays the market back in attention.").
  • Chiasmus — A-B-B-A reversal ("Risk taken for a reason becomes a reason taken for a risk.").
  • Zeugma — one verb governing two objects, one literal and one figurative ("She lost her phone, her sleep, and her concentration in a single term.").
  • Climax / cumulative listing — list ordered by escalating intensity ("The bedroom dim. The classroom drifting. The decade lost.").
  • Periodic sentence — the main point withheld until the end ("Despite the algorithms, the platforms, the engineered loneliness and the absent guidance — childhood survives.").
  • Cumulative sentence — main point first, then escalating detail ("The phone is the problem — the phone, the platform behind it, the algorithm behind the platform, and the boardroom behind the algorithm.").
  • Anastrophe — inverted word order ("Long the country has waited.").
  • Apostrophe — direct address to an absent person, idea or object ("Reader, you have done your share.").
  • Litotes — emphasis via understated negative ("This is not a small problem.").
  • Anadiplosis — last word of one clause becomes first word of the next ("We trust the screen. The screen trusts the algorithm. The algorithm trusts no one.").

The point. A top-band answer does not just use techniques — it picks the technique that fits the IDEA. Anaphora suits mounting pressure; asyndeton suits decisive action; chiasmus suits paradox; polysyndeton suits oppressive accumulation. Match the technique to the meaning.

Effects on the Reader — Sophicly's 4-fold framework

Every beat in IUMVCC has a target effect on the reader. At Sophicly we name four effects: Focus (what the writer makes the reader notice), Feel (the emotion gripping them — empathy and sympathy above all), Think (the judgement being reshaped), and Act (what they are moved to do).

  • I — Focus. What should the reader notice first?
  • U — Feel. What emotion grips them now?
  • M — Think. What argument is reshaping their judgement?
  • V — Feel + Act. What better future moves them?
  • C (counter) — Think. You have heard the objection; here is why it fails.
  • C (conclusion) — Act. The call that lands.

Name the Effect you are pursuing at every beat. Plans that only chase Think (logic) lose AO4 marks for failing to move the reader. When you reach for Feel, name a specific emotion — never a vague "a feeling".

Banned patterns — they cost AO4 and AO5 marks

  • Shows / showing / shown anywhere in the piece. Replace with illustrates, demonstrates, emphasises, reveals, exposes, presents, conveys, signals, enacts.
  • Abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to). Replace with a concrete image.
  • More than ten to be verbs across the piece (is / are / was / were). Use active verbs.
  • More than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.
  • Discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly … Secondly … Finally). Bridge with verbal connectors instead.
  • Triadic structures without parallel grammar.
  • Vague intensifiers (really, very, just, basically).
  • American register (move as a noun, gotten, trash). British English only.
  • Over-long sentences. Keep to roughly three lines (35–45 words) maximum — the reader, and the examiner, must be able to follow.

Persuasive craft — principles, not quotas

This is creative writing. There are no numeric rules — no fixed number of rhetorical questions, no set quota of anecdotes, no device confined to particular beats. Aristotle warned against persuasion by "scattered persuasive factors" (technique by count); judge by craft instead. The principles below are how to judge a choice, never boxes to tick.

  • Integrate the three appeals (Aristotle): ethos (why trust the writer?), pathos (what should the reader feel?), logos (what is the actual reason?) — woven together, not one technique repeated.
  • Fit the piece to its audience and occasion: a speech to peers is not a letter to a Minister is not a magazine article. Register is judged on AO4. Spec A's signature task is a speech to your peers responding to a statement of opinion — practise it most.
  • Concrete beats abstract: one specific image or named person moves a reader more than a general claim ("one boy, twelve, walks two hours for water", not "many people suffer").
  • Clarity is honesty (Orwell): prefer the short word, the active verb, the fresh image; cut every word that does no work.
  • Rhythm at the climax: repetition and three-part structures land hardest where the idea peaks — used by instinct, never to a count.
  • Call to action sits in the Conclusion. Make it specific (write to your MP, change one habit this week), not vague (make a change).
  • No author impersonation. Never write "in the voice of [a named real person]". Vary your openings through the hook archetypes only.

Q1 — Sport, competition, and what sport is for

Q1 — Sport, competition, and what sport is for

Why this is likely. Sport, competition, and values sit near the top of Sophicly's verified 2026 Spec A forecast, and a speech to peers on a statement of opinion is the signature Spec A second option. The topic is concrete, globally intelligible, and easy to anchor in verified figures on commercialisation and youth participation.

Three plausible stems (Edexcel prints ONE of these shapes):

  1. "Competition has poisoned sport. We have forgotten that the point of playing was the playing." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views on this statement.
  2. "Professional sport is no longer a sport; it is an entertainment industry that wears the badge of one." A magazine is publishing articles on the value of sport. Write your article.
  3. "School sport should be about taking part, not about winning." Write a letter to the editor of a newspaper giving your views.

Frame. The argument is not against money in sport, or against winning. The argument is against a version of competition that has forgotten what the game was for — that prices out the child, exhausts the professional, and confuses the scoreboard with the point.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Effect: Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Concrete contrast: "Picture two people. One earns, in a single Saturday afternoon, more than the other will earn coaching a children's team across the next decade."
  • Counterintuitive statistic: "Premier League ticket prices have tripled in real terms since 1989; the pitches the rest of us play on are closing."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Stoppage time. The board reads nine minutes. Both ends boo. The pitch belongs to neither of them."
  • Topic bridge: the game has been sold, and the love of it is being asked to pay rent.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Effect: Feel)

  • Image: sport as a tenant in its own stadium.
  • Metaphor: "The shirt is now a billboard the player is required to wear."
  • Extension: the kit changed every season; the children priced out of the seats their grandparents filled; the talented child whose Saturday club has closed.
  • Evidence: Premier League average ticket price tripling in real terms since 1989; Sport England Active Lives: Children and Young People Survey (2023/24) — 52% of children aged 5–16 in England do not meet the recommended 60 minutes of daily physical activity (the NHS recommends 60 minutes a day for under-18s).
  • Devices: triadic (kit / seat / pitch); short pivot ("This is not the game.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Effect: Think)

  • M1 — The professional. Frame: when the schedule never stops, the sport suffers. Metaphor: "We are wringing a sponge that has nothing left to give." Develop: expanded fixture lists; player burnout. Evidence: FIFPRO 2023 — elite players now compete in up to 70 matches a season.
  • M2 — The community. Frame: protect grassroots and the elite game self-corrects. Metaphor: "A river does not run if you concrete the source." Develop: council-pitch closures; participation fees that end a child's Saturday. Evidence: Sport England Active Lives 2023/24 (52% of 5–16 below the recommended 60 minutes of daily activity).
  • M3 — The values. Frame: teach the playing, not only the winning. Metaphor: "A scoreboard is a record, not a reason." Develop: school sport rebuilt around participation; the NHS 60-minutes-a-day target met through joy, not selection. Evidence: NHS activity guidance for under-18s.
  • Transitions — verbal bridges only: The professional is one cost. The community is the second. The values are what hold both. Never Firstly / Secondly / Finally.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Effect: Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (4 words): "Saturday afternoons return."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Children fill the pitches their grandparents walked off, free again."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Sport remembers itself."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Effect: Think) — three options

  • Excellence objection: "Competition is what produces excellence; remove it and standards collapse."
  • Joy objection: "Professional sport is the people's greatest shared joy; do not lecture it."
  • Realism objection: "Money is in every sport now; pretending otherwise is naïve."
  • Refutation pivot (works for all three): "There is competition that lifts the player, and there is commerce that empties the stand. Both wear the same shirt. Only one is the game."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Effect: Act)

  • Circular echo: the two people from the opening — the professional and the volunteer coach.
  • New move: the coach's pitch reopens; the child plays for free; the scoreboard becomes a record again, not a ransom.
  • Final sentence: "Sport was never the scoreboard. Give the game back to the people who play it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech to your peers, ~735 words) — opens with a concrete-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

Picture two people. One of them earns, in a single Saturday afternoon, more than the other will earn coaching a children's football team across the next ten years. They are not from different industries. They are from the same one. The same broadcast money, the same advertising, the same television-watching public pays for both — and yet the distance between the highest-paid professional and the parent who runs Saturday-morning training has grown, in real terms, into the distance between two separate economies. That distance is the subject of this speech. Competition did not have to do this to sport. But somewhere along the way, we let it.

[U — Urgency]

The shirt is now a billboard the player is required to wear. The kit changes every season; the seats fill with people who can afford the tripled prices, while the children who once filled them watch from home. Premier League ticket prices have more than tripled in real terms since 1989. At the same time, Sport England's 2024 Active Lives survey found that thirty-six percent of British five-to-sixteen-year-olds do not meet recommended activity levels — and the NHS recommends a full hour of activity a day for everyone under eighteen. The top of the pyramid has never been richer. The base has never been thinner. This is not the game.

[M1 — The professional]

We are wringing a sponge that has nothing left to give. FIFPRO reported in 2023 that elite players now compete in up to seventy matches a season — a schedule no human body was built to survive intact. The expanded competitions did not enrich the sport; they exhausted it. The player who limps off in April, after a season designed by accountants rather than coaches, is the visible cost of a calendar that treats athletes as content to be produced rather than people to be protected.

[M2 — The community]

A river does not run if you concrete the source. Protect grassroots sport, and the professional game corrects itself; starve it, and the talent dries up two decades later, quietly, where nobody is filming. Council-pitch closures and rising participation fees have ended Saturday football for thousands of children whose families simply cannot pay. Sport England's 2024 figures confirm the gap between richer and poorer postcodes is widening. The child priced out today is the player the country will miss in ten years.

[M3 — The values]

A scoreboard is a record, not a reason. We have taught a generation that sport is something you win rather than something you do, and in doing so we have driven away the very children the NHS most needs to be active for sixty minutes a day. Rebuild school sport around taking part, not around the team sheet, and you reach the child who would never have made the squad — the child whose health, not whose trophy cabinet, was the point all along.

[V — Vision]

Saturday afternoons return. Children fill the pitches their grandparents walked off, free again to play for the joy of it. Sport remembers itself.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say that competition is exactly what produces excellence — remove it, and standards collapse. There is truth in that. There is also this: there is competition that lifts the player, and there is commerce that empties the stand. Both wear the same shirt. Only one is the game. The fiercest contest on a free council pitch is still sport; the most expensive fixture played to price out its own supporters has started to become something else.

[C — Conclusion]

So, picture those two people one last time. The professional, paid a fortune for a Saturday. The volunteer coach, paid nothing for a decade. We have built a sport that celebrates one and forgets the other, and we have called the forgetting "competition". It is time we stopped. Reopen the pitch. Let the child play for free. Let the scoreboard go back to being a record rather than a ransom. Sport was never the scoreboard. Give the game back to the people who play it.

Alternative stems. The same IUMVCC shell answers Stem 2 (magazine article on whether professional sport is still a sport) and Stem 3 (letter to a newspaper editor on school sport and winning). For the article, keep the contrast hook; for the letter, open with the tripled-ticket statistic instead, and tighten the register to measured argument.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Billboard / tenant metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. No shows. No Americanisms.

Q2 — Extreme sports, risk, and why we chase it

Q2 — Extreme sports, risk, and why we chase it

Why this is likely. Risk and extreme activity is a recurrent Spec A article topic, and it pairs naturally with the non-fiction reading archive (mountaineering, adventure, survival writing). It rewards strong personal voice and a clear stance on a genuinely two-sided question.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Extreme sports are not bravery; they are vanity dressed as courage." A magazine is publishing articles on risk and adventure. Write your article giving your views.
  2. "We should encourage young people to take real, physical risks — the safe childhood is the dangerous one." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.
  3. "Adventure tourism puts unpaid rescuers in danger to satisfy the thrill-seeking of strangers." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your point of view.

Frame. Risk is not the problem; thoughtless risk is. The argument distinguishes the calculated risk that builds character from the performed risk that endangers others — and refuses to pretend that all danger is the same.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Shocking statistic: "In 2023, the volunteer teams of Mountain Rescue England and Wales answered 3,462 emergency calls — every one of them by an unpaid volunteer who left their own bed for a stranger's risk." (Mountain Rescue England and Wales Annual Review, 2024.)
  • Sensory snapshot: "Six hundred metres up. The rope holds. The climber's hands do not shake — they have been taught not to."
  • Bold statement: "There is brave risk, and there is borrowed risk. We have stopped telling them apart."
  • Topic bridge: the question is not whether to risk, but how, and at whose cost.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: the thrill industry as a salesman of other people's danger.
  • Metaphor: "Adrenaline is being sold by the ticket, and the rescue bill is sent to someone else."
  • Extension: the unprepared tourist; the volunteer rescuer pulled from bed; the family waiting below.
  • Evidence: Mountain Rescue England and Wales answered 3,462 calls in 2023 (Annual Review, 2024); all of its teams are unpaid and volunteer-run.
  • Devices: triadic (tourist / rescuer / family); short pivot ("This is not courage.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The calculated risk. Frame: trained, prepared risk builds the person. Metaphor: "Courage is risk that has done its homework." Develop: the discipline behind climbing, diving, mountaineering — preparation, not bravado. Evidence: the Duke of Edinburgh's Award reported in 2024 that participants showed measurable gains in life satisfaction and sense of purpose, and the Outward Bound Trust reports that around three-quarters of young people finish a course with greater confidence (both organisations' own impact research — attribute with "the organisations report").
  • M2 — The performed risk. Frame: risk for an audience is not the same as risk for growth. Metaphor: "A stunt filmed for strangers is a debt the stunt-maker does not pay." Develop: social-media-driven daredevilry; the pressure to film the dangerous thing. Evidence: argue from principle here rather than a statistic — the contrast between prepared risk and performed risk carries the point.
  • M3 — The shared cost. Frame: when risk is shared without consent, it stops being personal. Metaphor: "Your adventure is someone else's emergency." Develop: volunteer rescue services stretched; the duty of preparation. Evidence: the unpaid, volunteer basis of UK mountain and coastal rescue.
  • Transitions: Calculated risk is one thing. Performed risk is another. Shared cost is the line between them.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The young still climb high."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "They climb prepared, trained, and answerable for the danger they take on."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Courage grows up."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Freedom objection: "People have the right to risk their own lives; it is nobody else's business."
  • Character objection: "Risk is how the young become brave; wrap them in cotton wool and they grow up timid."
  • Economy objection: "Adventure tourism funds whole communities."
  • Refutation pivot: "Risk your own life, by all means — once you have made sure the bill for it does not arrive at a volunteer's door at three in the morning."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the trained hands that did not shake from the opening.
  • New move: the difference between the climber who prepared and the one who performed.
  • Final sentence: "Take the risk. Earn it first."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (magazine article, ~735 words) — opens with a shocking-statistic hook

[I — Introduction]

Every year, volunteer mountain rescue teams across England and Wales are called out thousands of times — and a striking share of those call-outs are for people who arrived on the mountain with no map, no training, and no plan beyond the photograph they intended to take. The exact proportion varies by region and by year, and the rescue services are careful not to shame the people they save. But the pattern is undeniable, and it raises a question this article wants to ask honestly: when we celebrate "extreme" risk, whose courage are we actually praising — and whose safety are we quietly spending? There is brave risk. There is borrowed risk. We have stopped telling them apart.

[U — Urgency]

Adrenaline is being sold by the ticket, and the rescue bill is sent to someone else. The unprepared tourist drives to the trailhead in trainers. The volunteer rescuer is pulled from bed in the dark. The family waits below for news. Almost all mountain and coastal rescue in this country is unpaid and volunteer-run, staffed by people who give up their nights to retrieve strangers from danger those strangers chose. This is not courage. This is courage outsourced to people who never agreed to supply it.

[M1 — The calculated risk]

And yet — courage is real, and risk is how the young find it. Courage is risk that has done its homework. The climber six hundred metres up whose hands do not shake has earned that steadiness through months of preparation; the diver who descends calmly has rehearsed every failure before it could happen. Programmes such as the Duke of Edinburgh's Award and Outward Bound have built exactly this kind of risk into the lives of young people for decades, and the principle is sound: prepared risk forms the person. The problem was never the height. The problem is the absence of preparation.

[M2 — The performed risk]

A stunt filmed for strangers is a debt the stunt-maker does not pay. There is a growing kind of risk that exists only for an audience — the dangerous thing done not to grow but to be watched, the cliff edge approached for the camera rather than the climb. The pressure to film the danger has turned preparation into an inconvenience and recklessness into content. Risk that builds you happens whether or not anyone is watching; risk performed for strangers happens because they are watching. The two could not be more different in what they cost.

[M3 — The shared cost]

Your adventure is someone else's emergency. The moment risk is taken without preparation, it stops being personal: it becomes a problem for the rescuer, the family, the community that funds the search. The volunteer who climbs into the storm to find you did not choose your adventure, yet pays for it in fear and exhaustion. Preparation is not the enemy of adventure. Preparation is the form respect for other people takes when you decide to climb.

[V — Vision]

The young still climb high. They climb prepared, trained, and answerable for the danger they take on. Courage grows up.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say that people have the right to risk their own lives, and that it is nobody else's business. In the abstract, that is fair. In practice, risk your own life, by all means — once you have made sure the bill for it does not arrive at a volunteer's door at three in the morning. The right to endanger yourself ends precisely where it begins to endanger the people who will be sent to save you.

[C — Conclusion]

So picture, one last time, the two pairs of hands: the climber who trained, whose hands did not shake, and the daredevil who performed, whose recklessness will summon a stranger from sleep. Both look brave in a photograph. Only one of them is. Extreme sport is not the enemy; thoughtless risk is. Take the risk. Earn it first.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (speech to peers defending real physical risk in childhood) by leading with M1, and Stem 3 (letter on adventure tourism endangering rescuers) by leading with M3. The shocking-statistic hook suits the article; for the speech, open with the bold-statement hook instead.

Q3 — How adults perceive teenagers

Q3 — How adults perceive teenagers

Why this is likely. The generational gap is one of the most reliable Spec A letter topics, and it pairs directly with the non-fiction reading archive's writing on youth, family, and changing times. It invites a confident, personal voice from a candidate who is themselves a teenager.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Today's teenagers are lazier, ruder, and more entitled than any generation before them." Write a letter to a newspaper responding to this statement.
  2. "Adults complain that teenagers spend their lives on screens. They forget who handed them the screens." Write a letter to an older relative giving your views.
  3. "Every generation believes the young are getting worse. Every generation is wrong." A magazine is running articles on the generation gap. Write your article.

Frame. Teenagers are not a worse version of their parents; they are a different generation facing different pressures, often invented and handed to them by the very adults who now criticise them. The reform target is the assumption, not the teenager.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Assumption-then-reversal: "You have been told that this generation of teenagers is the worst — the laziest, the rudest, the most distracted. You have heard it so often it sounds like a fact. It is the opposite of one."
  • Counterintuitive statistic: "NHS England's 2023 survey found that one in five young people aged 8 to 16 had a probable mental disorder — the supposedly 'lazy' generation is, in fact, a pressured one." (NHS England, Mental Health of Children and Young People in England, 2023.)
  • Rhetorical question: "When did being young become an accusation?"
  • Topic bridge: the complaint is older than the generation it accuses.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: the same complaint echoing down every generation.
  • Metaphor: "Every generation hands the next a world it broke, then complains about the cleaning-up."
  • Extension: the screens handed down; the pressures invented; the criticism that arrives faster than the support.
  • Evidence: Ofcom 2024 Children and Parents — 12–17-year-olds spend four-plus hours a day online (note: on devices adults designed and sold); NHS England's 2023 survey found one in five (20.3%) of 8–16-year-olds had a probable mental disorder, a proportion rising since 2017.
  • Devices: triadic (handed / invented / criticised); short pivot ("This is not laziness.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The inherited world. Frame: judge a generation by the world it was handed. Metaphor: "You cannot blame the swimmer for the tide you released." Develop: the climate, the economy, the housing market the young did not create. Evidence: principle, anchored to named pressures.
  • M2 — The invented pressure. Frame: the screen was a gift before it was a complaint. Metaphor: "The same hand that gave the phone now points at it." Develop: who designed, marketed and sold the devices; the adult industries profiting from teenage attention. Evidence: Ofcom 2024 online-time data.
  • M3 — The misread quiet. Frame: what looks like apathy is often pressure. Metaphor: "A tired generation is not a lazy one." Develop: academic load, anxiety, the cost of comparison. Evidence: NHS England's 2023 finding that one in five 8–16-year-olds had a probable mental disorder names the pressure the "laziness" charge ignores.
  • Transitions: The inherited world is one half. The invented pressure is the second. The misread quiet is the third.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The complaint becomes a conversation."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Adults ask what the young face before deciding they have failed."
  • Sentence 3 (4 words): "Two generations meet honestly."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Standards objection: "Some teenagers genuinely are rude and entitled; pretending otherwise helps no one."
  • Nostalgia objection: "Older generations worked harder and complained less; that is simply true."
  • Excuse objection: "Blaming adults for everything lets teenagers off the hook."
  • Refutation pivot: "There are rude teenagers, exactly as there were rude adults in every photograph of every past decade. The rudeness is not new. The willingness to define a whole generation by it is."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the accusation from the opening, now examined.
  • New move: the reader is asked to look again — at the world handed down, the screens passed on, the pressures invented.
  • Final sentence: "Before you decide we are worse, ask what we were given. Then ask who gave it."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (letter to a newspaper, ~735 words) — opens with an assumption-then-reversal hook

[I — Introduction]

Dear Editor,

You have been told that this generation of teenagers is the worst — the laziest, the rudest, the most entitled and distracted that any decade has produced. You have read it in headlines; you have heard it muttered on buses; you have perhaps believed it yourself. You have heard it so often that it has begun to sound like a fact. I am writing to tell you, as one of the teenagers being described, that it is the opposite of a fact. It is the oldest complaint in the world, and every generation that made it has been wrong.

[U — Urgency]

Every generation hands the next a world it broke, then complains about the cleaning-up. We were handed a warming planet, a housing market we cannot afford, and a set of glowing rectangles designed by adults to be impossible to put down. Ofcom's 2024 research found that twelve-to-seventeen-year-olds now spend more than four hours a day online — on devices that adults designed, adults marketed, and adults sold to us before we were old enough to consent. The criticism arrives faster than the support ever did. This is not laziness. This is a generation being blamed for a world it did not build.

[M1 — The inherited world]

You cannot blame the swimmer for the tide you released. Judge us, if you must, by the world we were handed: the climate emergency we did not cause, the economy we did not crash, the cost of a first home that has drifted out of reach of an entire generation's wages. We did not make these things. We inherited them. The young people you accuse of entitlement are, in fact, the least-entitled generation in living memory, because they are the first in a long time who expect to be poorer than their parents.

[M2 — The invented pressure]

The same hand that gave the phone now points at it. The screens that adults blame us for were a gift before they became a complaint. The platforms that hold our attention were engineered by grown adults in boardrooms, refined by grown adults in design teams, and sold to us by grown adults who profited from every minute. To hand a child a machine built to be addictive and then condemn the child for using it is not parenting. It is blame without responsibility.

[M3 — The misread quiet]

A tired generation is not a lazy one. What older eyes read as apathy is, far more often, exhaustion. The academic load is heavier, the comparison is constant, and youth mental-health services report rising pressure year on year. The teenager scrolling silently at the kitchen table is not refusing the world; very often, she is recovering from it. Mistaking that quiet for laziness is the most common error the older generation makes, and the least examined.

[V — Vision]

The complaint becomes a conversation. Adults ask what the young actually face before deciding they have failed. Two generations meet honestly.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some readers will say, fairly, that certain teenagers genuinely are rude and entitled, and that pretending otherwise helps nobody. They are right that such teenagers exist. There are rude teenagers, exactly as there were rude adults in every photograph of every past decade. The rudeness is not new. What is new is the willingness to define an entire generation by its worst members — a courtesy the older generation has never once extended to its own.

[C — Conclusion]

So before the next headline announces that the young are getting worse, I would ask one thing of your readers. Look again. Look at the world we were handed, the screens we were passed, the pressures that were invented and then blamed on us. Before you decide we are worse, ask what we were given. Then ask who gave it.

Yours faithfully,

[a teenager]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (letter to an older relative on screens) by tightening M2 into the whole piece, and Stem 3 (magazine article on the generation gap) by widening the historical frame in the introduction. The assumption-then-reversal hook suits all three.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Inherited-world / handed-down metaphor anchored I → U → M → Conclusion. NHS England 2023 youth mental-health figure verified (one in five of 8–16-year-olds). No shows. No vague intensifiers.

Q4 — Rules, values, and how we should behave

Q4 — Rules, values, and how we should behave

Why this is likely. Statements about rules, standards, and how to live are a Spec A staple for the speech-to-peers option, where Edexcel prints a bold value-claim and asks the candidate to agree or disagree. It rewards a clear position and a confident speaking register.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Rules are made to be broken. The people who change the world are the ones who refuse to follow them." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.
  2. "Good manners cost nothing and are worth everything. We are losing them, and we will miss them." Write the text for a speech to your peers giving your views.
  3. "Young people today have no respect for rules, traditions, or authority." Write a letter to a newspaper responding to this statement.

Frame. Rules are neither sacred nor worthless; the question is which rules earn obedience and which earn challenge. The mature position is not blind obedience or blanket rebellion, but judgement — the ability to tell a rule that protects from a rule that merely controls.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Bold statement: "Not all rules deserve to be followed. But not all rule-breaking deserves to be admired. The hard part — the only part that matters — is knowing the difference."
  • Rhetorical question: "Who decided that breaking a rule was the same as being brave?"
  • Contrast: "One rule kept a child safe last night. Another kept a good idea silent. We treat both as if they were the same thing."
  • Topic bridge: the question is not whether to follow rules, but which, and why.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: the difference between a fence and a cage.
  • Metaphor: "A good rule is a handrail; a bad rule is a leash."
  • Extension: the rule that protects the vulnerable; the rule that silences the questioner; the confusion that treats both with the same suspicion.
  • Evidence: argue from principle here, using uncontroversial historical examples — the just rule-breaking of Rosa Parks or the suffragettes set against reckless rule-breaking that endangers others. No statistic is needed; the contrast carries the argument.
  • Devices: triadic (handrail / leash / fence); short pivot ("This is not freedom.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The rule that protects. Frame: some rules are the accumulated wisdom of people who learned the hard way. Metaphor: "A safety rule is a scar someone else earned so you would not have to." Develop: rules that exist because someone was once hurt. Evidence: principle (road safety, safeguarding) — concrete, uncontroversial examples.
  • M2 — The rule that controls. Frame: some rules outlive their reason and survive only as habit. Metaphor: "A dead rule is a fence around an empty field." Develop: rules kept for tradition rather than purpose; the courage to ask "why". Evidence: historical reform examples (named, uncontroversial).
  • M3 — The judgement to tell them apart. Frame: maturity is not obedience or rebellion; it is discernment. Metaphor: "Wisdom is knowing which rules are walls and which are doors." Develop: how to question a rule responsibly — understand it before you break it. Evidence: principle-led.
  • Transitions: The protecting rule is one kind. The controlling rule is another. The judgement is what stands between them.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (4 words): "We keep the handrails."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "We question the leashes, carefully, knowing the difference before we act."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Judgement grows up."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Order objection: "If everyone decides which rules to follow, society collapses into chaos."
  • Authority objection: "Rules exist for a reason; questioning them is arrogance."
  • Rebellion objection: "All progress comes from breaking rules; caution is just cowardice."
  • Refutation pivot: "Choosing which rules to follow is not chaos; it is conscience. Chaos is following none. Cowardice is following all. Both are easier than the judgement in between."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the two rules from the opening — the one that protected, the one that silenced.
  • New move: the call to develop judgement, not obedience or rebellion.
  • Final sentence: "Do not break every rule. Do not keep every rule. Learn, instead, to tell them apart."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech to your peers, ~735 words) — opens with a bold-statement hook

[I — Introduction]

Not all rules deserve to be followed. But not all rule-breaking deserves to be admired. The hard part — the only part that actually matters — is knowing the difference between the two. We are told, constantly, that the people who changed the world were rule-breakers, and it is true that some of them were. But for every rule broken by someone brave, a hundred were broken by someone merely careless. This speech is not an argument for obedience, and it is not an argument for rebellion. It is an argument for the one thing that is harder than both: judgement.

[U — Urgency]

A good rule is a handrail; a bad rule is a leash. The trouble is that we have started to treat them as the same object. Somewhere a rule kept a child safe last night. Somewhere another rule kept a good idea silent. One protected; one controlled. And yet the slogan "rules are made to be broken" invites us to resent both of them equally, as though the handrail and the leash were enemies of the same kind. This is not freedom. This is the refusal to think about which is which.

[M1 — The rule that protects]

A safety rule is a scar someone else earned so that you would not have to. Almost every rule that protects a person exists because, at some point, a person was hurt without it. The road-crossing rule, the safeguarding rule, the rule that keeps the chemistry lab from becoming a hospital ward — none of these was invented by a tyrant. Each was written by experience, often painful experience, and handed forward so the next generation would not have to repeat the lesson. To break these rules is not rebellion. It is forgetting why they were written.

[M2 — The rule that controls]

But a dead rule is a fence around an empty field. Some rules have outlived every reason that justified them and survive only as habit — the policy nobody can explain, the tradition kept because changing it would require effort, the restriction whose only purpose is to remind you who is in charge. These rules deserve the question "why". History's genuine reformers were not people who broke rules at random; they were people who looked hard at one specific rule, understood exactly why it was wrong, and refused it on purpose.

[M3 — The judgement to tell them apart]

Wisdom is knowing which rules are walls and which are doors. Maturity is not the teenager who obeys everything, and it is not the teenager who defies everything; both have simply chosen the easier path. Maturity is the harder middle: understanding a rule fully before you decide whether it deserves you. The responsible way to challenge a rule is to learn it first — to know exactly what it protects against — and only then to argue, carefully, that the protection is no longer worth the cost.

[V — Vision]

We keep the handrails. We question the leashes, carefully, knowing the difference before we act. Judgement grows up.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say that if everyone simply decides which rules to follow, society collapses into chaos. It is a fair fear, but it misreads the argument. Choosing which rules to follow is not chaos; it is conscience. Chaos is following none of them. Cowardice is following all of them without thought. Both of those are easier than the judgement that sits in between — which is precisely why both are so common, and why the middle path is so rare.

[C — Conclusion]

So I will leave you with the two rules I began with. One kept someone safe. One kept someone quiet. They are not the same, and the slogans that tell you to break them both, or keep them both, are lying to you about how the world actually works. Do not break every rule. Do not keep every rule. Learn, instead, to tell them apart. That is not rebellion, and it is not obedience. It is growing up.

The bold-statement hook suits the speech; for the letter, open with the contrast hook instead.

Q5 — Social media and online identity

Q5 — Social media and online identity

Why this is likely. Social media, online identity, and the cost of a curated self are among the most current and most testable Spec A article topics. They pair with the non-fiction reading archive's writing on technology and modern life, and the verified statistics bank is unusually strong here.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Social media has not connected us; it has taught us to perform instead of to live." A magazine is publishing articles on life online. Write your article giving your views.
  2. "The version of yourself you post online is the only version most people will ever meet." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (magazine article, ~735 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

Social media is not a place; it is a stage — a vast, glowing, always-open theatre with no interval and no exit, where every user is at once the actor, the audience, the critic and the set designer, performing a version of a life to an auditorium that never empties and never sleeps. We were told it would connect us. In a sense, it has. But it has connected us as performers connect to a crowd — across footlights, in costume, reading from a script we did not write — rather than as people connect to one another. The performance has quietly replaced the person, and almost nobody noticed the swap.

[U — Urgency]

Every post is a small audition for a part the actor never chose to play. Ofcom's 2024 research found that twelve-to-seventeen-year-olds now spend more than four hours a day online — almost half their waking life outside school — and a large share of that time is spent not watching, but performing: choosing the filter, writing the caption, taking the photograph again because the first one did not lie convincingly enough. The comparison never sleeps. The self gets left behind the screen. This is not connection. This is a generation rehearsing a play it can never finish and never leave.

[M1 — The design]

We do not blame the swimmer for the riptide; we post lifeguards. The instinct, when teenagers struggle online, is to regulate the teenager — but the current was engineered. Infinite scroll, autoplay, streaks, the steady drip of small rewards designed to mask a single larger loss: each of these features was built by an adult design team to take and hold attention, and none of them is an accident. The Online Safety Act of 2023 was a first admission that the design, not the user, is where responsibility belongs. The reform target is the boardroom, not the bedroom.

[M2 — The literacy]

We gave a generation a printing press and never taught it to read. The deeper failure is not the platform; it is the silence around it. We handed children the most powerful persuasion machine ever built and offered almost no lessons in how it works — how the feed is ordered, how the comparison is manufactured, how the performance is rewarded. The Smartphone Free Childhood movement, founded by two British mothers in 2024, exists precisely because the instructions were never issued. Teach the literacy, and the tool stops owning the child.

[M3 — The self offline]

A self that is never offstage forgets how to rest. Jonathan Haidt's 2024 book The Anxious Generation argues that the rewiring of childhood around the performed online life is measurable in rising anxiety, and the remedy he points to is unglamorous: spaces where the phone goes away and the performance stops. The conversation that is not filmed, the afternoon that is not posted, the face that is not edited — these are not nostalgia. They are where the unperformed self is allowed to exist again.

[V — Vision]

The phone goes face-down. A generation learns to be seen without performing to be seen. The self returns.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say, in good faith, that social media connects lonely people to communities they could never have found offline. That is true. There is genuine connection on the platforms. There is also engineered loneliness around it. Both are real. Only one is profitable. The teenager who finds her community online has, somewhere beneath that discovery, often been made lonely first by the very architecture that now sells her the cure.

[C — Conclusion]

So picture the stage one last time — the footlights, the costume, the script no one chose. Then picture the lights going down. The actor steps off. The auditorium empties. And for the first time in hours, the person behind the performance is allowed to walk home, unwatched, unedited, and entirely real. That is the version of yourself worth protecting. Stop performing your life. Start living the one behind the screen.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (speech to peers on the posted self) by tightening M3 into the whole argument, and Stem 3 (letter arguing the problem is missing literacy) by leading with M2. The extended-metaphor opening suits the article and the speech; for the letter, open with the Ofcom statistic instead.

Q6 — School life and academic pressure

Q6 — School life and academic pressure

Why this is likely. Exam pressure, the length of the school day, and the cost of relentless assessment are recurrent Spec A topics, and the letter to a person in authority is a classic Section B form. The verified evidence on adolescent sleep is strong, current, and persuasive.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "We have built a school system that mistakes anxiety for ambition. The pressure is not making students better; it is making them ill." Write a letter to the head of your school giving your views.
  2. "Exams reward the people who can sit, focus, and finish — and school is where they learn it. Stop attacking them." A magazine is running articles on assessment. Write your article in response.
  3. "The school day starts too early for the teenage body. The science is settled, and we are ignoring it." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.

Frame. School is not the enemy of young people; the design of the school day is. A timetable built against teenage sleep, teenage attention, and teenage development punishes biology and calls the punishment ambition.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Measured definition: "A school day is, before anything else, a set of hours we have chosen to place between the moment a student wakes and the moment we ask her to learn. We chose those hours by inheritance, not by evidence."
  • Counterintuitive statistic: "The NHS recommends eight to ten hours of sleep a night for teenagers. The British school day is built on the assumption that they need six."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Half-past seven. The kitchen light is on. The fifteen-year-old chews toast without tasting it."
  • Topic bridge: rest is not the absence of learning; it is its precondition.

  • Image: the morning bell as an alarm fired into a sleeping body.
  • Metaphor: "We pull adolescents out of bed and call the daze discipline."
  • Extension: the lesson at 8:55 that nobody remembers by 10:30; the exam at 9:00 that decides a future at 9:48.
  • Evidence: NHS / Sleep Foundation recommend 8–10 hours; Crowley et al., Current Biology (2019), confirming adolescent circadian clocks run one to three hours later than adults'; Paul Kelly, University of Oxford (2019), reporting gains from a 10am start.
  • Devices: triadic (bell / lesson / exam); short pivot ("This is not teaching.").

  • M1 — The science. Frame: the teenage brain runs later than the adult brain. Metaphor: "A teenage body clock is not a broken adult clock; it is a different machine." Develop: the biological melatonin shift. Evidence: Crowley et al., Current Biology (2019).
  • M2 — The hours. Frame: a later start is alignment, not luxury. Metaphor: "Shift the start by ninety minutes and the same school becomes a different school." Develop: attendance and attainment gains. Evidence: Paul Kelly, Oxford (2019).
  • M3 — The assessment. Frame: the exam is not the enemy; the volume is. Metaphor: "Endless testing is not rigour; it is wear." Develop: reduce the quantity, keep the standard. Evidence: the 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review Interim Report (DfE, March 2025).
  • Transitions: The science is one story. The hours are the second. The assessment is the third.

  • Sentence 1 (4 words): "Lessons begin later."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Classrooms catch a brain that has had time to wake."
  • Sentence 3 (4 words): "Learning fits the body."

  • Resilience objection: "Adult life starts early; school should prepare students for it."
  • Logistics objection: "A later start inconveniences families and timetables."
  • Standards objection: "Less testing means lower standards and softer students."
  • Refutation pivot: "Preparing a student for adult life by exhausting her childhood is like preparing a runner by tiring her before the race. The exhaustion is not the training. It is the injury."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (letter to the head of your school, ~735 words) — opens with a measured-definition hook

[I — Introduction]

Dear Headteacher,

A school day is, before anything else, a set of hours — the hours we have chosen to place between the moment a student wakes and the moment we ask her to learn. In this school, as in almost every school in the country, we chose those hours by inheritance rather than by evidence: by the rhythm of an older economy, by habit, by the unspoken belief that a hard morning builds a strong character. These were not decisions about how teenagers learn. They were decisions about how the timetable has always run. I am writing to ask whether they are still the right ones.

[U — Urgency]

We pull adolescents out of bed and call the daze discipline. The NHS and the Sleep Foundation both recommend between eight and ten hours of sleep a night for teenagers; our school day is built on the quiet assumption that they manage on six. The lesson at 8:55 that nobody can recall by 10:30 is not a failure of teaching. The exam at 9:00 that decides a future at 9:48 is not a fair test of a brain that has not yet woken. This is not teaching. It is a timetable pitched against a body, and the body keeps losing.

[M1 — The science]

A teenage body clock is not a broken adult clock; it is a different machine. Crowley and colleagues, writing in Current Biology in 2019, confirmed that the adolescent body releases the sleep hormone melatonin one to three hours later than an adult's, and that this delay is biological — not laziness, not defiance, not a phone problem. The fifteen-year-old who cannot fall asleep at ten and cannot wake at seven is not failing at being disciplined. She is succeeding at being a teenager. We have known this for two decades. We continue to time the school day as if we had not.

[M2 — The hours]

A later start is not luxury; it is alignment. The University of Oxford's 2019 research into schools that shifted their start times reported measurable improvements in both attendance and attainment — the same students, in the same rooms, with the same teachers, learning better simply because their bodies had been allowed to wake. Ninety minutes on a clock face bought those schools a measurably different cohort. Ninety minutes is not a miracle. It is a decision, and it is one this school could take.

[M3 — The assessment]

The exam is not the enemy; the volume is. Endless testing is not rigour; it is wear. The Department for Education's 2025 Curriculum and Assessment Review Interim Report said in public what teachers have said in private for years: that the sheer breadth of testing dilutes rather than strengthens, and that the same standard can be assessed without grinding students down term after term. Reduce the quantity. Keep the standard. The two are not the same thing, and confusing them has cost this generation its rest.

[V — Vision]

Lessons begin later. Classrooms catch a brain that has had time to wake. The assessment timetable holds the same standard inside a calendar half the size. Learning fits the body.

[C — Counter-argument]

I anticipate the objection that adult life begins early, and that school should prepare students for it. But preparing a student for adult life by exhausting her childhood is like preparing a runner by tiring her out before the race begins. The exhaustion is not the training. It is the injury. A rested student is not a softer student; she is a student who can actually carry the discipline we are trying to teach her.

[C — Conclusion]

So I return to the half-past-seven kitchen, the toast eaten without tasting, the body asked to learn before it has woken. With one change — ninety minutes on a clock — that same student leaves later, arrives ready, learns properly, and carries the school day rather than merely surviving it. The change is small. The difference is a generation. Move the bell. The science is patient. The school years are not.

Yours sincerely,

[a student]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article defending exams) by inverting M3 — the volume becomes the discipline the writer values — and Stem 3 (speech to peers on the school start time) by leading with M1 and M2. The measured-definition opening works for the letter especially; for the speech, open with the NHS-sleep statistic instead.
