Q7 — Personal heroes and role models

Q7 — Personal heroes and role models

Why this is likely. Heroes, role models, and who deserves admiration is a warm, personal Spec A topic that suits the speech-to-peers form and rewards a genuine anecdote. It pairs with the non-fiction reading archive's biographical and reflective writing.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Real heroes are not the famous and the rich. They are the ordinary people who hold the world together quietly." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.
  2. "Celebrities make terrible role models, and we should stop pretending otherwise." A magazine is running articles on who young people look up to. Write your article.
  3. "The people who most deserve our admiration are the ones we never hear about." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.

Frame. We have confused fame with heroism. The people who genuinely hold the world together — the carers, the teachers, the quiet givers — receive the least attention precisely because their heroism does not perform. The reform target is who we choose to admire.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Personal anecdote: "When I was nine, the person who changed my life was not on a screen. She was a teacher who stayed twenty minutes late, every day, for a term, until I could read."
  • Bold statement: "We have confused the people we watch with the people we should become."
  • Rhetorical question: "When did we decide that the loudest life was the one most worth living?"
  • Topic bridge: heroism rarely trends; that is exactly why we miss it.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: the famous lit on a stage while the heroic works in the dark.
  • Metaphor: "We point the spotlight at the performer and leave the rescuer in shadow."
  • Extension: the carer who never trends; the volunteer nobody films; the teacher whose name no one outside one classroom will ever know.
  • Evidence: argue from principle here, anchored to concrete figures — the carer, the volunteer, the teacher — rather than a survey statistic. The contrast between the celebrated and the unseen carries the point.
  • Devices: triadic (carer / volunteer / teacher); short pivot ("This is not heroism.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The quiet hero. Frame: heroism is daily, not dramatic. Metaphor: "Courage is mostly a small thing done again the next day." Develop: the carer, the teacher, the parent working two jobs. Evidence: principle, anchored to a concrete example.
  • M2 — The borrowed hero. Frame: fame is not the same as worth. Metaphor: "A famous face is a mirror, not a map." Develop: why we mistake visibility for value; the cost of admiring performance. Evidence: argue from principle here — the famous face we mistake for a role model versus the unglamorous figure who actually shaped us — rather than a survey statistic.
  • M3 — The reachable hero. Frame: the best role models are the ones you can actually become. Metaphor: "You cannot follow a footprint you will never reach." Develop: choosing models whose lives are imitable; learning from people you can meet. Evidence: the Sutton Trust's research on aspiration and social mobility (a real, named body) supports the point that reachable role models shape ambition; argue the principle rather than quoting a specific figure.
  • Transitions: The quiet hero is one kind. The borrowed hero is another. The reachable hero is the one worth choosing.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "We admire what we can become."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The carer, the teacher, the quiet giver finally receive their light."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Heroism returns home."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Aspiration objection: "Famous role models inspire ambition; ordinary ones do not."
  • Reach objection: "Celebrities reach millions; a local hero reaches a handful."
  • Realism objection: "Young people will always admire the famous; this is just how it is."
  • Refutation pivot: "A famous life can inspire you to want; only a reachable life can teach you how. Wanting without a path is not aspiration. It is longing."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the teacher from the opening, or the chosen quiet hero.
  • New move: the call to look at the people already around us.
  • Final sentence: "Stop watching the famous. Start thanking the heroic — and then become one."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech to your peers, ~735 words) — opens with a personal-anecdote hook

[I — Introduction]

When I was nine years old, the person who changed my life was not on a screen. She was a teacher, and she stayed twenty minutes late every single day, for an entire term, until a boy who could not read could. There was no audience for this. No one filmed it. No one will ever write her biography. And yet I am standing here today, able to give this speech, largely because of her — and I have never once seen her name trend. This speech is about the people the spotlight forgets, and why they are the only heroes worth the word.

[U — Urgency]

We point the spotlight at the performer and leave the rescuer in shadow. Ask most people to name a hero and they will name someone famous — a footballer, a singer, a face from a feed — because fame is loud and heroism is quiet, and we have grown used to mistaking the volume for the value. Meanwhile the carer who never trends, the volunteer nobody films, and the teacher whose name no one outside one classroom will ever learn keep the actual world running. This is not heroism as we have been taught to see it. But it is the only kind that has ever held anything together.

[M1 — The quiet hero]

Courage is mostly a small thing done again the next day. The carer who returns each morning to a job that exhausts her; the parent working two jobs so a child can sit an exam in peace; the teacher who stays late without being asked — none of these performs a single dramatic act. They perform an ordinary one, repeatedly, when no one is watching and nothing rewards it. That repetition, not the dramatic gesture, is what heroism actually looks like. The drama is what films well. The repetition is what saves people.

[M2 — The borrowed hero]

A famous face is a mirror, not a map. When we admire the famous, we are usually admiring their visibility rather than their worth — confusing the size of an audience with the size of a life. The footballer is not more heroic than the carer because he is watched by millions; he is simply watched. Fame measures attention, not virtue, and a generation that learns to admire attention will spend its life chasing the spotlight rather than the substance behind it.

[M3 — The reachable hero]

You cannot follow a footprint you will never reach. The best role model is not the most famous one; it is the one whose life you could actually live. The teacher, the nurse, the local volunteer, the older student two years ahead of you — these people are heroes you can study up close, question, imitate, and one day equal. A distant celebrity can make you want a different life. Only a reachable hero can show you, step by ordinary step, how to build one.

[V — Vision]

We admire what we can become. The carer, the teacher, the quiet giver finally receive their light. Heroism returns home.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say that famous role models inspire ambition in a way ordinary ones cannot — that a child needs a star to dream of. There is something in that. But a famous life can inspire you to want; only a reachable life can teach you how. Wanting without a path is not aspiration; it is longing. The star tells you that greatness exists. The teacher down the corridor tells you how to walk towards it.

[C — Conclusion]

So I will end with the teacher who stayed late, the one whose name will never trend, the one without whom I could not have spoken to you today. She is not unusual. There is one like her in every life in this room — a quiet hero already standing nearby, unfilmed and unthanked. Stop watching the famous. Start thanking the heroic. And then, when it is your turn, become one.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (article on celebrities as role models) by leading with M2, and Stem 3 (letter on the unheard heroes) by tightening M1 into the whole piece. The personal-anecdote hook suits the speech; for the article, open with the bold-statement hook instead.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Spotlight / shadow metaphor anchored I → U → V. Argues from concrete example rather than a survey figure. No shows.

[I — Introduction]

When I was nine years old, the person who changed my life was not on a screen. She was a teacher, and she stayed twenty minutes late every single day, for an entire term, until a boy who could not read could. There was no audience for this. No one filmed it. No one will ever write her biography. And yet I am standing here today, able to give this speech, largely because of her — and I have never once seen her name trend. This speech is about the people the spotlight forgets, and why they are the only heroes worth the word.

[U — Urgency]

We point the spotlight at the performer and leave the rescuer in shadow. Ask most people to name a hero and they will name someone famous — a footballer, a singer, a face from a feed — because fame is loud and heroism is quiet, and we have grown used to mistaking the volume for the value. Meanwhile the carer who never trends, the volunteer nobody films, and the teacher whose name no one outside one classroom will ever learn keep the actual world running. This is not heroism as we have been taught to see it. But it is the only kind that has ever held anything together.

[M1 — The quiet hero]

Courage is mostly a small thing done again the next day. The carer who returns each morning to a job that exhausts her; the parent working two jobs so a child can sit an exam in peace; the teacher who stays late without being asked — none of these performs a single dramatic act. They perform an ordinary one, repeatedly, when no one is watching and nothing rewards it. That repetition, not the dramatic gesture, is what heroism actually looks like. The drama is what films well. The repetition is what saves people.

[M2 — The borrowed hero]

A famous face is a mirror, not a map. When we admire the famous, we are usually admiring their visibility rather than their worth — confusing the size of an audience with the size of a life. The footballer is not more heroic than the carer because he is watched by millions; he is simply watched. Fame measures attention, not virtue, and a generation that learns to admire attention will spend its life chasing the spotlight rather than the substance behind it.

[M3 — The reachable hero]

You cannot follow a footprint you will never reach. The best role model is not the most famous one; it is the one whose life you could actually live. The teacher, the nurse, the local volunteer, the older student two years ahead of you — these people are heroes you can study up close, question, imitate, and one day equal. A distant celebrity can make you want a different life. Only a reachable hero can show you, step by ordinary step, how to build one.

[V — Vision]

We admire what we can become. The carer, the teacher, the quiet giver finally receive their light. Heroism returns home.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say that famous role models inspire ambition in a way ordinary ones cannot — that a child needs a star to dream of. There is something in that. But a famous life can inspire you to want; only a reachable life can teach you how. Wanting without a path is not aspiration; it is longing. The star tells you that greatness exists. The teacher down the corridor tells you how to walk towards it.

[C — Conclusion]

So I will end with the teacher who stayed late, the one whose name will never trend, the one without whom I could not have spoken to you today. She is not unusual. There is one like her in every life in this room — a quiet hero already standing nearby, unfilmed and unthanked. Stop watching the famous. Start thanking the heroic. And then, when it is your turn, become one.

Q8 — Travel, tourism, and cultural experience

Q8 — Travel, tourism, and cultural experience

Why this is likely. Travel and cultural experience is a natural Spec A article topic — globally intelligible, two-sided, and rich in verified figures. It pairs with the non-fiction reading archive's strong tradition of travel writing.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Tourism is quietly destroying the very places it claims to love." A magazine is publishing articles on travel and its costs. Write your article giving your views.
  2. "Travel broadens the mind. Every young person should see as much of the world as they can." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.
  3. "We should travel less, and travel deeper. The age of the cheap, careless holiday must end." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your point of view.

Frame. Travel itself is not the problem; the assumption that we are entitled to anywhere on the planet at any cost is. The argument is for travel that respects the place rather than consuming it.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Extended metaphor: "Tourism in 2026 is a machine of staggering scale and untracked appetite — a billion-traveller engine that has outgrown every mechanism built to contain it."
  • Counterintuitive statistic: "International tourist arrivals passed 1.4 billion in 2023 — a return to pre-pandemic peaks, at the very moment several of the world's most-loved cities declared themselves overwhelmed." (UNWTO, 2024)
  • Sensory snapshot: "August. Three thousand passengers step off a single cruise ship onto an island built for eight hundred."
  • Topic bridge: travel has become consumption with a passport.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: the destination as a sandcastle the tide of travellers is washing out.
  • Metaphor: "The famous view now waits in a queue that begins in another country."
  • Extension: rents priced out of locals' reach; the bakery that became a souvenir shop; the city printed in a language its residents do not speak.
  • Evidence: UNWTO 2024 — 1.4bn arrivals 2023; Amsterdam's 2023–24 tourism cap; Eurostar 2024 record rail demand.
  • Devices: triadic (rent / bakery / menu); short pivot ("This is not travel.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The traveller. Frame: travel slower, fewer times, closer. Metaphor: "A holiday is not a trophy." Develop: rail over short-haul flights; one considered trip over five careless ones. Evidence: Eurostar 2024 record demand.
  • M2 — The destination. Frame: protect the place with limits. Metaphor: "A loved city needs a turnstile, not a tide." Develop: timed entry; tourist taxes redirected to housing. Evidence: Amsterdam 2023–24 tourism cap.
  • M3 — The respect. Frame: travel as guest, not consumer. Metaphor: "A guest leaves the house as she found it." Develop: learning before arriving; spending locally; treating culture as host, not backdrop. Evidence: principle-led.
  • Transitions: The traveller is one decision. The destination is the second. The respect is what holds both.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (6 words): "The street is quiet on August Tuesdays."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Locals walk to bakeries that still bake; rents fall to what wages can carry."
  • Sentence 3 (3 words): "Place outlasts visit."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Livelihood objection: "Tourism is the livelihood of millions; limit it and they suffer."
  • Liberty objection: "Telling people not to travel is moralism dressed as policy."
  • Exchange objection: "Travel is how the world becomes less narrow."
  • Refutation pivot: "Tourism is a livelihood — which is exactly why it should not be left to collapse under its own weight. Cap the volume, raise the per-visitor contribution, and the livelihood is preserved by the very thing currently destroying it."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the island from the opening; the cruise ship docking once a week, not seven days a week.
  • New move: the island's residents still earn; the island is still itself.
  • Final sentence: "Travel less. Travel deeper. Pay the place its rent."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (magazine article, ~735 words) — opens with an extended-metaphor hook

[I — Introduction]

Tourism in 2026 is a machine of staggering scale and untracked appetite — a billion-traveller engine of cruise ships and short-haul jets and platform-rented apartments and migrating itineraries, a system of arrivals and departures that has, in a single generation, scaled past every regulatory mechanism we ever built to contain it. It is now so large that the destinations it celebrates are being measurably worn away by the celebration itself. In the summer of 2025, three thousand passengers stepped off a single cruise ship onto an island built for eight hundred. That is the machine. That is the appetite. And the places it loves are paying for the love.

[U — Urgency]

The famous view now waits in a queue that begins in another country. Rents in central Lisbon, Amsterdam, Athens and Barcelona have been priced out of locals' reach by short-stay platforms; restaurant menus are printed in English; the bakery that used to be a bakery has become a souvenir shop. The UN World Tourism Organization recorded over 1.4 billion international tourist arrivals in 2023 — a full return to pre-pandemic peaks, at the very moment several of the world's most-visited cities had already declared themselves overwhelmed. This is not travel. This is the slow consumption of place by the people who came to admire it.

[M1 — The traveller]

A holiday is not a trophy. Travel slower, travel fewer times, travel closer. Eurostar's 2024 figures show passenger numbers on the London–Paris route at record levels, with a carbon footprint per passenger a fraction of the equivalent flight. One considered trip, planned for and remembered, gives a household more than five careless short-haul holidays a year — and the difference is not only in the carbon. It is in the trip itself, restored to what it was meant to be: an event, anticipated and savoured, rather than another entry ticked off a calendar.

[M2 — The destination]

A loved city needs a turnstile, not a tide. Amsterdam's 2023–24 city policy banned new hotels in the centre, capped cruise-ship arrivals, and redirected tourist-tax revenue into housing for residents. These are not anti-tourism measures; they are pro-place measures. They protect the city the visitor came to see by ensuring it is still recognisably itself by the time the visitor arrives. A destination loved to death helps no one — least of all the next traveller, who finds only the ruins of what the brochure promised.

[M3 — The respect]

A guest leaves the house as she found it. The deepest change is not a policy at all; it is a posture. The traveller who learns a little of the language before she arrives, who spends in local shops rather than international chains, who treats the culture as a host rather than a backdrop, takes nothing the place cannot spare. Travel becomes a relationship rather than an extraction. The visitor who arrives as a guest, not a consumer, is the one a destination can actually afford to welcome.

[V — Vision]

The street is quiet on August Tuesdays. Locals walk to bakeries that still bake; rents fall to what wages can carry. Place outlasts visit.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some will say tourism is the livelihood of millions, and that limiting it punishes the very people who depend on it. It is true that millions depend on it — which is exactly why it should not be left to collapse under its own weight. Cap the volume, raise the per-visitor contribution, redirect the proceeds into the housing the local workforce can no longer afford, and the livelihood is preserved by the very policies that look, on the surface, like restrictions. The alternative is the destination's quiet death, and the livelihood dies with it.

[C — Conclusion]

The machine runs. The destinations bear it. They cannot bear it indefinitely. Cap the volume, raise the contribution, redirect it to the local rents the visitor's arrival has quietly raised, and the engine throttles back to the rate at which a place can still recognise itself. The island where three thousand once disembarked now welcomes a ship a week, and its eight hundred residents still earn, still bake, still belong. Travel less. Travel deeper. Pay the place its rent.

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (speech to peers defending travel) by leading with M3 and reframing the argument as respectful travel rather than no travel, and Stem 3 (letter on travelling less and deeper) by leading with M1. The extended-metaphor opening suits the article; for the speech, open with the UNWTO statistic instead.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Machine / appetite and sandcastle / tide metaphors anchored I → U → V. No shows. No abstract concept nouns. No Americanisms.

Q9 — The environment and individual responsibility

Q9 — The environment and individual responsibility

Why this is likely. Climate and individual responsibility is one of the most current and most testable Spec A topics, suiting the speech-to-peers form especially well. The verified statistics bank is strong, and the topic is globally intelligible.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "The climate is not somebody else's problem. Every household, every choice, every ordinary day is where it is decided." Write the text for a speech to your peers expressing your views.
  2. "Recycling, electric cars and meat-free meals will not save the planet. The problem is industrial, not personal." A magazine is running articles on climate action. Write your article in response.
  3. "It is governments, not individuals, who failed the climate. Stop guilting ordinary families." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.

Frame. Individual climate action without policy is performance, but policy without citizen pressure is decoration. The Sophicly position holds both at once: the household votes with its choices, but the choice that matters most is the vote at the ballot box.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Repetition / anaphora: "We choose. We refuse. We rebuild. Three small verbs, repeated by enough households on enough ordinary mornings, are what the climate has been waiting for."
  • Bold statement: "There is no household innocent of the climate, and no household powerless before it."
  • Sensory snapshot: "Forty degrees in July. The tarmac softens. The radio reads a record temperature and the country drives, anyway, to the beach."
  • Topic bridge: the climate is not a crisis to feel; it is a posture to take.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: climate change as a slow flood already inside the home.
  • Metaphor: "The flood is not coming. It is in the hallway."
  • Extension: the record summer; the off-track target; the family pretending nothing.
  • Evidence: Met Office 2022 — the UK passed 40°C for the first time on record; Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report — the UK is off track for its 2030 targets; Carbon Brief on consumption emissions.
  • Devices: triadic (record / target / denial); short pivot ("This is the room.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The household. Frame: the smallest unit of climate policy is the kitchen. Metaphor: "Your fridge is a small parliament." Develop: the meat-free week, the thermostat, the boycotted brand. Evidence: WRAP 2024 — over 2.2 million tonnes of plastic packaging a year.
  • M2 — The street. Frame: street-level pressure changes councils. Metaphor: "Pavements are policy waiting to happen." Develop: community schemes; the waste no one recycles. Evidence: UNEP 2023 — only around 9% of all plastic ever made has been recycled.
  • M3 — The ballot box. Frame: the vote is the loudest action a household can take. Metaphor: "A ballot is the household's longest lever." Develop: pressing representatives on the 2030 pathway. Evidence: Climate Change Committee 2024 progress report.
  • Transitions: The kitchen is one step. The street is the next. The ballot is the last.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The street has trees again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off."
  • Sentence 3 (2 words): "We breathe."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Corporate-only objection: "Individual action is a distraction; only industry matters."
  • Wealth objection: "The richest can afford solar panels; the poorest cannot, so it is unfair."
  • Despair objection: "One country, one household, changes nothing."
  • Refutation pivot: "The company answers to the consumer it cannot ignore; the government answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Withdraw the consumer and the company moves. Withdraw the voter and the regulator vanishes. Both move together, or neither moves."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the three verbs from the opening, now enacted.
  • New move: the household has chosen; the street has chosen; the ballot has chosen.
  • Final sentence: "We choose. We refuse. We rebuild. The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (speech to your peers, ~735 words) — opens with a repetition / anaphora hook

[I — Introduction]

We choose. We refuse. We rebuild. Three small verbs — and I want to convince you, in the next few minutes, that these three verbs, repeated by enough households on enough ordinary mornings, are exactly what the climate has been waiting for. We have been told that the climate is somebody else's problem: the government's, the corporation's, the next generation's. It is not. It is decided in the kitchen, on the street, and in the polling booth, by people who look a great deal like the people in this room. The climate is not a crisis to feel helpless about. It is a posture to take.

[U — Urgency]

The flood is not coming. It is in the hallway. In 2022, the Met Office confirmed that the United Kingdom had passed forty degrees Celsius for the first time in recorded history. In 2024, the Climate Change Committee told Parliament that the country is no longer on track to meet its own legally binding 2030 emissions targets. The record summer arrived; the target slipped; and the temptation, in every household, is to look at the rising water and pretend the carpet is dry. This is the room. The water is already in it. The only question left is what we do while standing in it.

[M1 — The household]

Your fridge is a small parliament. Every meal is a vote, and sixty-five million households voting three times a day is a supply chain the nation cannot ignore. A meat-free week shifts measurable carbon; a thermostat set lower lets the power station breathe out a little less; a brand refused at the checkout is a signal a company genuinely fears. WRAP reported in 2024 that the United Kingdom places more than two million tonnes of plastic packaging on the market every year — and almost all of it begins in a household decision. The kitchen, multiplied, is policy in disguise.

[M2 — The street]

Pavements are policy waiting to happen. The recycling we are so proud of is, on its own, almost a comfort: the United Nations Environment Programme reported in 2023 that only around nine percent of all the plastic ever made has actually been recycled. The bin is not the answer. But the street is — the community scheme, the local petition, the pressure that begins on a pavement and ends in a council chamber. Street-level action is the most underestimated political force this country owns, precisely because it looks, in its early weeks, like nothing.

[M3 — The ballot box]

A ballot is the household's longest lever. The kitchen and the street do their work, but the ballot box reaches furthest — it is the only mechanism that can drag the country back onto the 2030 pathway the Climate Change Committee has measured us against. Press the people who represent you. Vote on the climate plans of those who ask for your support. The corporation answers to the consumer it cannot ignore, and the regulator answers to the voter it cannot avoid.

[V — Vision]

The street has trees again. Children walk to a school that learnt to switch its lights off. We breathe.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some of you will say that individual action is a distraction — that only industry can move the numbers that matter. There is truth in this, and a harder truth beneath it: the company answers to the consumer it cannot ignore, and the government answers to the voter it cannot avoid. Pull the consumer, and the company moves; pull the voter, and the regulator wakes. The two are not rivals. Both move together, or neither moves at all.

[C — Conclusion]

So I come back to the three verbs I began with. We choose — what we eat, what we buy, what we refuse. We refuse — the waste, the excuse, the comfortable belief that this is somebody else's emergency. We rebuild — the street, the school, the country, one ordinary morning repeated until the curve finally bends. The science is patient. The decade is not. We choose. We refuse. We rebuild. The climate listens to small rooms. Speak from yours.

The same shell answers Stem 2 (article arguing the problem is industrial) by sharpening the counter-argument into the spine of the piece, and Stem 3 (letter rejecting the household-blame frame) by leading with the ballot-box beat. The repetition hook suits the speech; for the article, open with the Met Office statistic instead.

Q10 — Appearance, image, and judgement

Q10 — Appearance, image, and judgement

Why this is likely. Body image, appearance, and the cost of judgement is a current, well-evidenced Spec A topic, and the letter to a newspaper is a classic Section B form for it. It pairs with the non-fiction reading archive's writing on identity and modern pressures.

Three plausible stems:

  1. "Filters have stolen a generation's mirror. Young people now compare themselves to faces that do not exist." Write a letter to a newspaper giving your views.
  2. "Stop telling young people to love their bodies. Tell the industries that profit from their insecurity to stop." Write a letter to a magazine giving your point of view.
  3. "We should not judge anyone on their appearance. So why does almost every conversation begin with it?" A magazine is running articles on image. Write your article.

Frame. The body is not the problem. The mirror that an industry built — and that an algorithm now keeps polishing — is the problem. The reform target is the design and the seller, not the child being sold to.

I — INTRODUCTION (60–110 words · Focus)

  • Hook — pick one:
  • Historical contrast: "A teenager in 1900 looked into a piece of glass that had no commercial interest in her dissatisfaction. A teenager in 2026 looks into a mirror engineered by a multi-billion-pound industry that depends on it."
  • Counterintuitive statistic: "The Mental Health Foundation found that more than a third of UK adults have felt anxious because of their body image — and that the figure rises above half among teenagers."
  • Sensory snapshot: "The fourteen-year-old leans into the mirror, then leans out, then opens the camera app, then leans in again — this time at the face the app prefers."
  • Topic bridge: the comparison is industrial.

U — URGENCY (110–160 words · Feel)

  • Image: the filter as a quiet seller at the bathroom door.
  • Metaphor: "We are raising a generation in a hall of mirrors built by people they will never meet."
  • Extension: the photo retaken because the first did not lie enough; the comparison that never sleeps.
  • Evidence: Mental Health Foundation 2019 — 34% of UK adults anxious about body image, rising above half among teenagers; Norway's Marketing Control Act 2021 (edited-image disclosure); the Dove Self-Esteem Project; PSHE Association guidance.
  • Devices: triadic (changing room / lunch queue / bathroom photo); short pivot ("This is not vanity.").

M — METHODOLOGY (280–380 words · Think)

  • M1 — The platform. Frame: regulate the filter, not the child. Metaphor: "Label the edited photo the way we label the doctored one." Develop: disclosure rules; the right to know what is real. Evidence: Norway's Marketing Control Act 2021.
  • M2 — The classroom. Frame: teach the trick. Metaphor: "Reveal the seam in the costume and the costume stops convincing." Develop: media-literacy lessons; live editing demonstrations. Evidence: the Dove Self-Esteem Project.
  • M3 — The vocabulary. Frame: give the child the words to name the lie. Metaphor: "A child who can name the trick is a child the trick cannot fully hold." Develop: media literacy in tutor time; conversation scripts. Evidence: PSHE Association guidance on body image.
  • Transitions: The platform is one fix. The classroom is the second. The vocabulary is the third.

V — VISION (110–160 words · Feel + Act)

  • Sentence 1 (5 words): "The mirror is honest again."
  • Sentence 2 (10–12 words): "The fourteen-year-old leaves the room thinking about lunch, not her face."
  • Sentence 3 (5 words): "Youth reclaims its own face."

C — COUNTER-ARGUMENT (80–110 words · Think) — three options

  • Always-was objection: "Young people have always worried about their looks; the filter is just the new mirror."
  • Free-speech objection: "Regulating filters and adverts is censorship."
  • Confidence objection: "Confidence-building, not regulation, is the answer."
  • Refutation pivot: "There is a mirror, and there is a billion-pound industry standing behind that mirror with a sales pitch. The worry is not new. The seller is."

C — CONCLUSION (80–110 words · Act)

  • Circular echo: the two mirrors from the opening — the honest glass and the engineered one.
  • New move: the call to tell the child the difference, and to regulate the seller.
  • Final sentence: "Give the mirror back to the child. The child is enough."

Model answer — written to Stem 1 (letter to a newspaper, ~735 words) — opens with a historical-contrast hook

[I — Introduction]

Dear Editor,

A teenager in 1900 looked at herself in a dressing-table mirror her mother had bought. The mirror told her, more or less, the truth — slightly flattering at one angle, slightly unkind at another, but it was a piece of glass, and it had no commercial interest in her dissatisfaction. A teenager in 2026 looks at herself in a mirror engineered by a multi-billion-pound industry whose entire business model depends on her dissatisfaction. The filter is not a piece of glass. It is an industry standing behind a piece of glass with a sales pitch. The two mirrors are not the same, and I am writing because we are pretending they are.

[U — Urgency]

We are raising a generation in a hall of mirrors built by people they will never meet. The Mental Health Foundation's 2019 body-image report found that more than a third of UK adults had felt anxious because of their body image, and that the figure rose above half among teenagers. That research is now several years old, and the technology has only accelerated since. The changing room before sport; the lunch queue; the bathroom photograph retaken because the first one did not lie convincingly enough — each of these ordinary moments has industrialised. This is not vanity. This is a comparison engineered, deliberately, to fail.

[M1 — The platform]

Regulate the filter, not the child. We should label the edited photograph the way we already label the doctored advertisement. Norway amended its Marketing Control Act in 2021 to require influencers and advertisers to disclose when an image of a body has been digitally altered; the rest of Europe is still deciding whether to follow. The teenager scrolling tonight has no way of knowing which faces are real and which are built — and the platforms that profit from her not knowing are not the ones we should be asking to teach her better. The image-makers must disclose. The child does not need re-educating; the sellers do.

[M2 — The classroom]

Reveal the seam in the costume and the costume stops convincing. The Dove Self-Esteem Project, which delivers body-image lessons in over a hundred and fifty countries, has demonstrated that teaching teenagers exactly how a filtered image is made — what is removed, what is added, what is smoothed, what is simply impossible — measurably reduces the harm those images cause. A single lesson, taught with a phone and a screen and a live editing demonstration, can take the power out of a thousand feeds. The classroom can do what the platform refuses to.

[M3 — The vocabulary]

A child who can name the trick is a child the trick cannot fully hold. Parents and teachers cannot out-shout the algorithm, but they can hand the young person the words to recognise it. PSHE Association guidance on media literacy and body image gives any tutor a starting script — how to ask, how to listen, how to name what the screen is doing. A short, regular conversation, repeated across a year, will not defeat the industry, but it will leave the teenager with a vocabulary the industry cannot take back. Naming the lie is the beginning of surviving it.

[V — Vision]

The mirror is honest again. The fourteen-year-old leaves the room thinking about lunch, not her face. Youth reclaims its own face.

[C — Counter-argument]

Some readers will say that young people have always worried about their looks, and that the filter is merely the newest mirror. But there is a mirror, and there is a billion-pound industry standing behind that mirror with a sales pitch. The worry is not new. The seller is. To pretend the difference between a dressing-table glass and a global, profit-engineered comparison engine is a difference of degree, rather than of kind, is to misread the entire problem.

[C — Conclusion]

So I return to the two mirrors. One was honest glass. One is engineered to sell. The teenager looking into the second does not always know the difference; the industry behind it always does. We can either tell her — and regulate the seller standing behind the glass — or we can leave her with the comparison and hope the next decade is kinder than this one. The mirrors are not the same. Give the mirror back to the child. The child is enough.

Yours faithfully,

[a concerned reader]

Alternative stems. The same shell answers Stem 2 (letter to a magazine on the industries profiting from insecurity) by anchoring M1 around regulatory pressure on those industries, and Stem 3 (article on judging by appearance) by widening the introduction into everyday conversation. The historical-contrast hook suits the letter; for the article, open with the sensory-snapshot hook instead.

Voice + banned-patterns check. Hall-of-mirrors metaphor anchored I → U → Conclusion. Industry / social-pressure / commercial-design framing throughout (no banned framings). No shows. No abstract concept nouns.

Cross-question reuse table — memorise the recombinations

The ten plans above share underlying moves. The same metaphor frame, hook archetype, or refutation pivot reappears across multiple Qs. Memorise the recombinations and you can rebuild any plan from any other on the day.

Move Used in Reusable in
Billboard / tenant / sold-game framing Q1 (Sport) Q8 (Tourism), Q10 (Appearance)
Borrowed-risk / outsourced-cost framing Q2 (Extreme sports) Q9 (Environment), Q1 (Sport)
Inherited-world / handed-down framing Q3 (Generations) Q6 (School pressure), Q9 (Environment)
Handrail / leash / judgement framing Q4 (Rules) Q3 (Generations), Q7 (Heroes)
Stage / performance metaphor Q5 (Social media) Q10 (Appearance), Q7 (Heroes)
Different-machine / different-clock framing Q6 (School pressure) Q5 (Social media), Q10 (Appearance)
Spotlight / shadow framing Q7 (Heroes) Q5 (Social media), Q1 (Sport)

Flood-in-the-hallway / small-room framing Q9 (Environment) Q8 (Tourism), Q6 (School pressure)
Hall-of-mirrors / industrial-comparison framing Q10 (Appearance) Q5 (Social media), Q3 (Generations)

If exam time is tight, rehearse Q1, Q5, and Q9 first — the three with the highest 2026 probability score on the Spec A forecast, and the three that exercise the signature speech-to-peers form. For broader coverage rehearse all ten. The shells are deliberately recombinant: the worst-case scenario is that Edexcel prints a stem you have not rehearsed; the best-case scenario is that the metaphor frame you rehearsed for one theme fits the stem Edexcel actually printed. Both scenarios are designed for. Remember that you answer only one of Q6 or Q7 on the day — choose the task whose form you have practised most, not the topic that merely looks easier.

Creative stretches — one extreme example per Q

The ten skeletons above are the SAFE shape — IUMVCC, six beats, the locked structure that consistently scores high AO4 for organisation. They will land your answer firmly in the upper bands.

To push into the very top of AO4, the examiner needs to see voice. Not just techniques — voice. The examples in The Complete Writer's Craft show the range: a conversational, self-interrupting piece that thinks aloud and is rigorously argued beneath the loose surface; an argument made entirely through contrast; a piece narrated by an unexpected speaker. None of these is a template to copy. They are proof that the opening can be anything, provided it earns its place.

The rule for breaking the rules. A creative stretch only works if the voice / form choice fits the topic. Always ask: does this voice ARGUE the topic, or does it just decorate it? If the voice argues, ship it. If it decorates, cut it. Never reach for a clever voice because it impressed you somewhere else — reach for the one this question needs. And never impersonate a named real person; the inventive voice must be an archetype or an object, not a celebrity.

Below, one stretch idea per Q. Use them as inspiration; do NOT mimic them verbatim.

Q1 — Sport: write as a single stadium seat across the decades

Stretch idea. Personify one seat — Row K, Seat 14 — and let it narrate the supporters who have sat in it across forty years: the 1985 occupant who paid little and queued in the rain; the 2025 occupant who paid a fortune and watched half the match through a phone screen. The seat notices what has changed about who can afford to sit in it.

Why it works. The object-narrator (apostrophe — a top-band technique) makes the abstraction "commercialisation" physical: the seat stays the same; the price, the crowd and the meaning change around it.

Risk to manage. Keep the seat's voice restrained — it observes, it does not lecture. The moment it starts reciting statistics in its own voice, the conceit breaks.

Q2 — Extreme sports: write as a mountain rescue logbook

Stretch idea. Frame the article as a series of entries in a volunteer rescue team's logbook across a single weekend — each call-out a short, factual entry, the human cost building quietly between the lines. The final entry breaks format to address the reader directly.

Why it works. The understated logbook register makes the argument by accumulation rather than assertion. The dry factuality is more damning than any plea.

Risk to manage. The entries must stay clipped and unemotional until the final break. Sentiment in the early entries kills the effect.

Q3 — Generations: write as a letter answered by a letter

Stretch idea. Open with three short paragraphs in the voice of an exasperated adult writing about "young people today" — then have the teenage writer answer it, line by line, in the rest of the piece.

Why it works. The two-voice structure stages the generation gap rather than merely describing it; the rebuttal lands harder because the accusation is right there on the page.

Risk to manage. The adult voice must be plausible, not a strawman. A cartoonish opening makes the rebuttal feel cheap.

Q4 — Rules: write as two signs on the same wall

Stretch idea. Open with two notices imagined side by side — one a genuine safety warning, one an absurd outdated restriction — and let the speech argue from the gap between them.

Why it works. The paired image makes the central distinction (the rule that protects versus the rule that controls) instantly visible before a single claim is made.

Risk to manage. Keep the two signs tight and parallel. If the joke about the absurd rule runs long, the argument becomes a sketch.

Q5 — Social media: write the first half as a perfect post, then break character

Stretch idea. Write the opening as a flawless, glossy social-media caption — the curated, performed self at full volume — then have the real voice cut in: "That took me forty minutes to write. None of it is true."

Why it works. The meta move demonstrates the argument instead of describing it: the gap between the performance and the person is enacted on the page.

Risk to manage. The glossy opening must be convincingly hollow. If it accidentally reads as sincere, the break loses its force.

Q6 — School pressure: write as a timetable that speaks

Stretch idea. Personify the school day itself — the timetable narrating its own hours, defending the early start, growing slowly aware that the body it governs is exhausted.

Why it works. Personifying the structure makes "the school day" stop being an abstract noun and start being a character that can be questioned and changed.

Risk to manage. The timetable must not become whimsical. Keep its voice institutional and a little weary.

Q7 — Heroes: write as an award no one collects

Stretch idea. Frame the speech around an imaginary awards ceremony for the unthanked — the carer, the teacher, the volunteer — none of whom turns up, because each is busy doing the heroic thing the award is for.

Why it works. The empty stage makes the point physically: real heroism is too busy holding the world together to attend its own celebration.

Risk to manage. Keep the conceit from turning sentimental. The empty chairs should feel like quiet truth, not melodrama.

Q8 — Travel: write as the destination itself

Stretch idea. Personify an over-visited city. Write the article as a letter FROM the city TO the world: "I have stood here for fifteen centuries. I am being loved to death. Please choose differently."

Why it works. Apostrophe (direct address from a personified speaker) is a top-band technique. Personifying the place makes "over-tourism" a body rather than an abstract noun — and bodies persuade.

Risk to manage. Sustained personification is a marathon; the student voice cannot leak in until the final call to action.

Q9 — Environment: write as a household inventory

Stretch idea. Structure the speech around a single household's ordinary objects — the fridge, the thermostat, the bin, the ballot paper — each one made to testify, in turn, to the climate decision it quietly represents.

Why it works. Anchoring the argument in physical objects keeps the abstract topic concrete, and the inventory format gives the piece a clear, inventive organisation.

Risk to manage. Keep each object's section short. The inventory must build momentum, not catalogue endlessly.

Q10 — Appearance: write as the filter

Stretch idea. Persona-shift. Write the letter from the point of view of a beauty filter, narrating its day shaping the faces of every teenager who opens the app — cheerful, professional, slightly proud — until the closing admission that it has never met, and will never meet, a single one of them.

Why it works. The instrument of the harm narrates itself. The filter's cheerful obliviousness is the indictment.

Risk to manage. The filter's voice must not slide into the writer's. It does not moralise — it SELLS. The argument lands by what the filter is too oblivious to see.

How to use these stretches in the exam

  • Do not attempt a stretch unless you have rehearsed it once. Cold improvisation under exam pressure is risky.
  • The IUMVCC beats stay. Even the most inventive persona piece still has an introduction, an argument, a counter-argument, and a conclusion. The persona is the surface; the structure underneath is unchanged.
  • One stretch only. Pick one frame; sustain it.
  • If the topic does not fit, ship the safe skeleton. A solid upper-band answer beats a confused, half-finished experiment every time — and on Spec A, an under-length experiment is doubly penalised.

Final pre-submission checklist

Before you put the pen down, run this checklist in your head. Every item costs marks if it slips.

  • ✓ Six IUMVCC beats present and named in order.
  • ✓ Word count 700 or above — aim for 720–760. Under 700 is the single most common way Spec A candidates lose AO4 marks. There is no upper cliff, but past ~800 control starts to slip.
  • ✓ You answered only one of Q6 / Q7, not both.
  • ✓ Register matched to the form and audience the stem named (speech to peers ≠ letter to authority ≠ magazine article). AO4 judges this directly.
  • ✓ Hook from one of the nine archetypes — not a generic opening.
  • ✓ One extended metaphor anchored across at least three beats (I → U → Conclusion at minimum).
  • ✓ Triadic structure used with parallel grammar in U and Conclusion at minimum.
  • ✓ Conclusion echoes the Introduction image (circular structure rewards AO4 organisation).
  • ✓ Direct address (you, we, us) used for solidarity, not scattered mechanically.
  • ✓ A specific named emotion in U (Feel) — not a vague "a feeling".
  • ✗ No shows / showing / shown.
  • ✗ No abstract concept nouns (the importance of, the situation with, the necessity to).
  • ✗ No discourse-marker scaffolding (Firstly, Secondly, Finally).
  • ✗ No really, very, just, basically.
  • ✗ No Americanisms (move as a noun, gotten, trash).
  • ✗ Fewer than ten to be verbs (is, are, was, were) — this protects your AO5 mark for varied, active grammar.
  • ✗ No more than five or six imperatives across the whole piece.
  • ✗ No sentence longer than roughly three lines — long sentences are where AO5 punctuation errors hide.

Tick every line. Section B is forty-five marks — twenty-seven for the writing (AO4), eighteen for the accuracy (AO5). The pen is the only thing between you and them.
